zotero.abstractNote: Selective State-Space Models (SSMs) have recently emerged as a powerful alternative to Transformers for sequence modeling, achieving state-of-the-art performance on long-range tasks. Despite their empirical success, a rigorous theoretical understanding of their capabilities and limitations remains elusive. In this work, we provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis of deep selective SSMs. We first show that selective SSMs can efficiently approximate any sequence-to-sequence function that can be represented by a broad class of recurrent neural networks, including those with high-order interactions. We then establish that selective SSMs can solve long-range dependency tasks that are provably hard for standard linear SSMs. Finally, we provide a theoretical justification for the selection mechanism, showing that it allows the model to adaptively focus on relevant parts of the input sequence, effectively performing a form of dynamic memory management. Our results provide a solid foundation for the design and analysis of future selective SSM architectures.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 38
